=E2=80=9CCautious But Tenacious=E2=80=9D: The Shari Saga Continues

Larry and David Ellison, along with Gerry Cardinale and RedBird Capital, ar=
e back at the table, trying once again to make a deal with Shari Redstone f=
or Paramount Global. The Paramount stock is up 18 percent this week, includ=
ing 10 percent alone on Wednesday. Shareholders clearly want a deal at this=
 point.

The Ellisons and RedBird have the first part of the deal with Shari inked: =
They have agreed to buy National Amusements Inc., the family holding compan=
y that controls 77 percent of the voting stock of Paramount Global, for $1.=
75 billion=E2=80=94up a bit from the previous offer of $1.7 billion, which =
I also believe is a net number to Shari and her family. In other words, the=
 Ellisons and RedBird have also agreed to provide to Shari an additional ne=
arly $400 million, or more, that she needs to pay off the $200
million or so in bank debt at the NAI level and the $175 million, in the fo=
rm of a PIK preferred, that NAI owes to its banker, Byron Trott, and his fi=
rm, BST & MSD Partners.

The fact that the Ellisons and the Redstones are talking again is, itself, =
a positive sign, since communications between the two families broke down w=
eeks before the previous iteration of this deal fell apart. I suspect that =
Larry Ellison, with a net worth these days of $157 billion, played a bigger=
 role behind the scenes getting things with Shari unstuck than has been sha=
red to date. In any event, part one of the complicated deal looks like it=
=E2=80=99s back on track.

That leaves the special committee with the enviable task of evaluating the =
second part of the Ellison/RedBird deal for its =E2=80=9Cfairness from a fi=
nancial point of view=E2=80=9D to the Class B nonvoting shareholders of Par=
amount Global. I=E2=80=99m told the terms of the Ellison/RedBird deal are b=
asically unchanged from the
previous offer that the special committee never really got to consider in a=
ll its glory and detail. That would mean an injection of $1.5 billion to pa=
y down Paramount=E2=80=99s $12.2 billion of net debt, in order to elevate i=
t to investment grade. Plus there will be an additional $4.5 billion offere=
d to the non-Redstone shareholders, spread across all the shareholders who =
elect to sell, on a pro-rata basis. I=E2=80=99m told that will amount to ro=
ughly $7.50 a share in cash, or maybe a little less, for the non-Redstone s=
hareholders, plus an ongoing ownership stake in the still-public Paramount.=
 The final piece of the puzzle is that Paramount would then buy Skydance Me=
dia, Ellison=E2=80=99s Hollywood production company, for $4.75 billion in s=
tock. Ellison/RedBird could then bring in David and The Jeffs (Shell and Zu=
cker) to run the combined company, with the notion of tripling or quadrupli=
ng the company=E2=80=99s stock price over time=E2=80=94a sweetener for some=
 shareholders to
stay in the deal.

So, in short, a complicated arrangement with several moving parts. But Shar=
i appears to have a deal for NAI that she can live with, and the other Para=
mount shareholders=E2=80=94some of whom have voting rights and some of whom=
 do not=E2=80=94would get a still somewhat amorphous combination of cash an=
d stock for their existing shares, which they can opt into or not. One thin=
g that is not on the table anymore, even as a negotiating point, is any kin=
d of majority of the minority vote, which would have given either voting sh=
areholders or all shareholders a chance to approve or veto the deal. In oth=
er words, if Shari and the special committee ratify the deal, that=E2=80=99=
s it=E2=80=94no shareholder votes, no proxy statements need be filed, etcet=
era. The deal would contain a 45-day =E2=80=9Cgo-shop=E2=80=9D provision, w=
hich would allow for another buyer to come along with a better offer and to=
p the agreed-upon Ellison deal.

ous but tenacious=E2=80=9D in trying to get this over the finish line this =

SCRIBERS+%287%2F3%2F24%29&utm_content=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%286%2F14=
%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c6=
0617da42abd12a ) that they=E2=80=99d actually been aware of Biden=E2=80=99s=
 physical decline for some time, but only then felt license to cover it fre=
ely. =E2=80=9CAnyone who covers this White House knows he=E2=80=99s showing=
 the signs of his age=E2=80=94he whispers, he shuffles, he misremembers,=E2=
=80=9D one White House reporter told me. =E2=80=9CThe amount of time we spe=
nt talking about it versus the time we spent reporting on it was not the sa=
me,=E2=80=9D said another. =E2=80=9CThere should have been tougher, more sc=
rutinizing
coverage of his age earlier.=E2=80=9D

Of course, the Hur report was four and a half months ago, and whatever lice=
nse journalists finally felt to focus on Biden=E2=80=99s acuity during that=
 news cycle obviously subsided until Biden put his challenges on display on=
 Thursday, with no filter, before an audience of 51 million Americans. And =
that, perhaps, highlights the true handicap of the
White House press corps, and arguably the media at large: It is too often r=
eactive instead of proactive, waiting for news to happen rather than forcin=
g an issue, allowing the people it covers to set the agenda rather than set=
ting the agenda itself.

Indeed, the rapid developments of the past days=E2=80=94the leaked Obama co=
mments, the Times story about Biden weighing the decision, the grumblings a=
mong governors and on the Hill=E2=80=94suggest that this dynamic has revers=
ed itself. The Bidens have declared their intention to stay the course. The=
 only factors that will undermine the decision are lousy polls, of course, =
and tenacious, relentless journalism.

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;"><b>Larry</b> and <b=
>David Ellison</b>, along with <b>Gerry Cardinale</b> and RedBird Capital, =
are back at the table, trying once again to make a deal with <b>Shari Redst=
one </b>for Paramount Global. The Paramount stock is up 18 percent this wee=
k, including 10 percent alone on Wednesday. Shareholders clearly want a dea=
l at this point. <br/><br/>The Ellisons and RedBird have the first part of =
the deal with Shari inked: They have agreed to buy National Amusements Inc.=
, the family holding company that controls 77 percent of the voting stock o=
f Paramount Global, for $1.75 billion=E2=80=94up a bit from the previous of=
fer of $1.7 billion, which I also believe is a <i>net number</i> to Shari a=
nd her family. In other words, the Ellisons and RedBird have also agreed to=
 provide to Shari an additional nearly $400 million, or more, that she need=
s to pay off the $200
million or so in bank debt at the NAI level and the $175 million, in the fo=
rm of a PIK preferred, that NAI owes to its banker, <b>Byron Trott</b>, and=
 his firm, BST & MSD Partners. <br/><br/>The fact that the Ellisons and=
 the Redstones are talking again is, itself, a positive sign, since communi=
cations between the two families broke down weeks before the previous itera=
tion of this deal fell apart. I suspect that <b>Larry Ellison</b>, with a n=
et worth these days of $157 billion, played a bigger role behind the scenes=
 getting things with Shari unstuck than has been shared to date. In any eve=
nt, part one of the complicated deal looks like it=E2=80=99s back on track.=
 <br/><br/>That leaves the special committee with the enviable task of eval=
uating the second part of the Ellison/RedBird deal for its =E2=80=9Cfairnes=
s from a financial point of view=E2=80=9D to the Class B nonvoting sharehol=
ders of Paramount Global. I=E2=80=99m told the terms of the Ellison/RedBird=
 deal are basically unchanged from the
previous offer that the special committee never really got to consider in a=
ll its glory and detail. That would mean an injection of $1.5 billion to pa=
y down Paramount=E2=80=99s $12.2 billion of net debt, in order to elevate i=
t to investment grade. Plus there will be an additional $4.5 billion offere=
d to the non-Redstone shareholders, spread across <i>all</i> the shareholde=
rs who elect to sell, on a pro-rata basis. I=E2=80=99m told that will amoun=
t to roughly $7.50 a share in cash, or maybe a little less, for the non-Red=
stone shareholders, plus an ongoing ownership stake in the still-public Par=
amount. The final piece of the puzzle is that Paramount would then buy Skyd=
ance Media, Ellison=E2=80=99s Hollywood production company, for $4.75 billi=
on in stock. Ellison/RedBird could then bring in David and <b>The Jeffs </b=
>(<b>Shell</b> and <b>Zucker</b>) to run the combined company, with the not=
ion of tripling or quadrupling the company=E2=80=99s stock price over time=
=E2=80=94a sweetener for some shareholders to
stay in the deal. <br/><br/>So, in short, a complicated arrangement with se=
veral moving parts. But Shari appears to have a deal for NAI that she can l=
ive with, and the other Paramount shareholders=E2=80=94some of whom have vo=
ting rights and some of whom do not=E2=80=94would get a still somewhat amor=
phous combination of cash and stock for their existing shares, which they c=
an opt into or not. One thing that is not on the table anymore, even as a n=
egotiating point, is any kind of <i>majority of the minority vote</i>, whic=
h would have given either voting shareholders or all shareholders a chance =
to approve or veto the deal. In other words, if Shari and the special commi=
ttee ratify the deal, that=E2=80=99s it=E2=80=94no shareholder votes, no pr=
oxy statements need be filed, etcetera. The deal would contain a 45-day =E2=
=80=9Cgo-shop=E2=80=9D provision, which would allow for another buyer to co=
me along with a better offer and top the agreed-upon Ellison deal. <br/><br=
/>Could things go wrong? Will <b>Charles
Phillips</b>, the former Oracle executive and former Morgan Stanley banker =
who seems to have become the leading voice on the special committee, thwart=
 the deal, or complicate it, or make new demands of Ellison/RedBird? (Phill=
ips did not respond to my penetrating questions about what the special comm=
ittee will do at this point. It is a holiday week, after all.) Could Shari =
get cold feet, change her mind in Sun Valley, throw her support back behind=
 the Pep Boys, or be seduced by <b>Diller</b>, <b>Bronfman</b>, or the ling=
ering Apollo/Sony option? I=E2=80=99m told that the parties involved are be=
ing =E2=80=9Ccautious but tenacious=E2=80=9D in trying to get this over the=
 finish line this time. One thing is for sure, though, as long-suffering Pa=
ramount voting shareholder <b>Mario Gabelli</b> tweeted on Tuesday night, q=
uoting the legendary dealmaker <b>Yogi Berra</b>, =E2=80=9CParamount=E2=80=
=A6it ain=E2=80=99t over till it=E2=80=99s over=E2=80=A6hmmm.=E2=80=9D <br/=
><br/><i>And now to the main event=E2=80=A6</i><br/></td>

 onslaught of questions from the White House press corps about President <b=
>Biden</b>=E2=80=99s mental fitness, his acuity, and whether he suffers fro=
m =E2=80=9Cany form of dementia,=E2=80=9D CNN correspondent <b>M.J. Lee</b>=
 asked press secretary <b>Karine Jean-Pierre</b> if the administration woul=
d release more comprehensive medical records for the incumbent. =E2=80=9CYo=
u=E2=80=99ve said a couple of times now that the White House has provided t=
horough medical records for the president,=E2=80=9D Lee said. =E2=80=9CI=E2=
=80=99m not sure that was a full account, necessarily.=E2=80=9D Biden=E2=80=
=99s doctor had described him as =E2=80=9Ca healthy, active, robust 81-year=
-old male who remains fit to successfully execute the duties of the preside=
ncy.=E2=80=9D But, noted Lee, =E2=80=9CI think that is clearly not what the=
 majority of Americans are seeing.=E2=80=9D Predictably, Jean-Pierre counte=
red that the medical
records the administration had already released were, in fact, very compreh=
ensive.<br/><br/>Inside CNN, I=E2=80=99m told, the network=E2=80=99s White =
House team had determined that it would use its allotted time at every brie=
fing for the foreseeable future to press the administration for more offici=
al documentation on Biden=E2=80=99s health=E2=80=94and, indeed, Lee followe=
d up with the same question at the briefing today. Presumably, <i>every</i>=
 news organization with a seat in the Brady Briefing Room has established i=
ts own variation on the theme=E2=80=94the angle by which it hopes to break =
the dam of Jean-Pierre=E2=80=99s calm and collected filibuster and advance,=
 however incrementally, what is obviously the <i>only</i> story on the beat=
 right now. For Biden campaign reporters, of course, the line of questionin=
g has a more tactical insinuation: What is the fate of his reelection effor=
t, and will he be at the top of the ticket come Chicago, or even next week?=
 In either case, it=E2=80=99s clear the Biden press
corps has the bit between its teeth. <br/><br/>That is their professional o=
bligation and duty. But it=E2=80=99s also impossible to ignore the very pal=
pable frustration underlying their inquiry. Since taking office, and especi=
ally during the campaign, the Biden press team had largely succeeded in mak=
ing any scrutiny around the principal=E2=80=99s age taboo=E2=80=94an issue =
that was not only indiscreet to discuss, according to them, but also irrele=
vant to his governance and, of course, the existential specter of <b>Trump<=
/b>=E2=80=99s return to the White House and, with it, the erosion of democr=
acy itself. <br/><br/>The old pros in the press shop were often quick to pi=
vot to Biden=E2=80=99s historic legislative accomplishments and record. If =
a journalist <i>did</i> cover Biden=E2=80=99s age, they would be forced to =
reckon with the coordinated (and often petty) wrath of his press handlers, =
who aggressively tried to shame them off the subject. =E2=80=9CThey can be =
coordinated and vitriolic in their efforts if they
don=E2=80=99t like a story,=E2=80=9D one White House correspondent told me.=
 =E2=80=9CAnd then there is often a coordinated effort to trash individual =
reporters who manage to glimpse behind the veil.=E2=80=9D <br/><br/>In the =
wake of last Thursday=E2=80=99s debate, there is now a pervasive sense of r=
esentment among the press corp toward the administration. =E2=80=9CThere is=
 hostility that has been brewing for a while because the White House has be=
en so dismissive of this for a long time,=E2=80=9D one White House reporter=
 said. Of course, the resentment comes in different flavors. Reporters who =
<i>did </i>prosecute questions around Biden=E2=80=99s mental fitness and to=
ok plenty of shit for it=E2=80=94journalists at<i> The New York</i> <i>Time=
s</i>, Axios, <i>The</i> <i>Wall Street Journal</i>, etcetera=E2=80=94feel =
a strong sense of vindication that has only reinforced their suspicions abo=
ut the duplicitousness of the communications office. Meanwhile, many of the=
 journalists who accepted the official line that age was <i>not </i>an
issue, or at least not an important issue, are now undeniably apoplectic=E2=
=80=94chafing at the White House comms office for waving them off what is n=
ow <i>inarguably</i> a <i>massive</i> and truly <i>historical </i>issue=E2=
=80=94or covering their tracks. =E2=80=9CNot only do they feel lied to by t=
he White House, but they got their asses kicked on The Big Story,=E2=80=9D =
one White House reporter told me. =E2=80=9CThat=E2=80=99s a potent combinat=
ion of being pissed off.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>So potent are the feelings over =
this issue, in fact, that there=E2=80=99s been some <i>intra</i>-press corp=
s friction, as well. Multiple reporters who have been on the receiving end =
of White House pushback on the age topic have suspected that the press offi=
ce subtly recruited their peers to assist their campaign. (Sounds gross, bu=
t alas, P.R. people use similar tactics all the time, as you all well know.=

 Republican lawmakers sounded the alarm about Biden=E2=80=99s mental
acuity, some White House reporters took to X to rebut the premise of the st=
ory, effectively aligning with the position of the White House itself. <br/=
><br/>=E2=80=9CThat is a frequent White House tactic they have used with re=
porters,=E2=80=9D one White House journalist said, =E2=80=9Cand some report=
ers oblige.=E2=80=9D At the same time, some reporters are rolling their eye=
s at colleagues who appear to be taking a victory lap over their pre-debate=
 scrutiny of Biden=E2=80=99s age, noting that not <i>all </i>of it was nece=
ssarily great reportage. (=E2=80=9CThat <i>Journal </i>story <i>was </i>fuc=
ked,=E2=80=9D one reporter said.)<br/></td>
